record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (deeds and mortgages recorded at county level)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Municipal clerk or registrar (town-level registration from 1848 statewide)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1848 (statewide vital registration — one of the earliest states). Earlier records at township/church level.

burned_county_authority: New Jersey has no burned counties. Record survival is generally excellent across all 21 counties. The oldest counties (Bergen, Burlington, Essex, Middlesex, Monmouth, Salem — all from the 1680s) have continuous deed and probate records from formation. The most significant loss was the 1873 Monmouth County courthouse fire, which destroyed some court records but left deed and probate records intact. New Jersey's dual colonial heritage (East Jersey and West Jersey) creates a complex but well-documented proprietary land record system.
administrative_notes: New Jersey was one of the 13 original colonies. Originally divided into East Jersey and West Jersey (united as a royal colony in 1702). The East Jersey/West Jersey distinction is critical for land records research: East Jersey Proprietors' records cover Bergen, Essex, Middlesex, Monmouth, and parts of Somerset, Hunterdon, Morris, Sussex, Passaic, and Union Counties; West Jersey Proprietors' records cover Burlington, Salem, Gloucester, Cumberland, Cape May, Camden, Atlantic, Ocean, Mercer, Warren, and parts of Hunterdon and Somerset Counties. The New Jersey State Archives (NJSA) in Trenton is the central repository for state-level records. County clerks record deeds and mortgages; Surrogates handle probate. Statewide vital registration began in 1848, making NJ one of the earliest states for civil vital records. New Jersey was the last Northern state to abolish slavery — the 1804 Gradual Abolition Act freed children born after July 4, 1804, but some enslaved persons were held as 'apprentices for life' until the 13th Amendment (1865).

core_repositories.description: The single most important genealogical repository in New Jersey. Holds colonial records, East Jersey and West Jersey Proprietors' records, vital records (births, marriages, deaths from 1848 and earlier), military records, court records, naturalization records, tax ratables, slave registrations, wills/inventories (pre-1901 copies), and state government records. The archives has an extensive microfilm collection and finding aids.

Founded in 1845, NJHS is the state's premier manuscript repository. Holds over 2 million manuscripts including family papers, business records, maps, photographs, and the 'Proceedings of the New Jersey Historical Society' (from 1845). The library includes published genealogies, county histories, and the NJ Archives series (colonial documents).

Major NJ manuscript repository holding colonial papers, East Jersey Proprietors' minute books, NJ political figures' papers, and extensive NJ-specific collections. The Sinclair New Jersey Collection is one of the largest assemblages of NJ printed and manuscript materials. Also holds Alexander Library's genealogy reference collection.

core_repositories.source_name: Gardner A. Sage Library — New Brunswick Theological Seminary
core_repositories.repository: Gardner Sage Library, 21 Seminary Place, New Brunswick
core_repositories.description: Houses the archives of the Reformed Church in America, including Dutch Reformed church records from NJ's colonial congregations. Baptism, marriage, and membership records — many in Dutch — are essential for colonial NJ genealogy, especially for Bergen, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth, and Hunterdon Counties.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive indexed and browsable NJ collections including vital records, probate records, county clerk records, church records, tax lists, and military records. FamilySearch partnered with NJSA to digitize key record series.

familysearch_collections.source_name: FamilySearch Wiki — New Jersey Research Guide
familysearch_collections.description: Comprehensive research guide covering NJ resources by record type and county. Includes repository information, record availability, and research strategies specific to NJ's East Jersey/West Jersey dual heritage.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / NJ Digital Highway
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized NJ newspapers including the Newark Daily Advertiser, Trenton State Gazette, Elizabeth Journal, Paterson Press, and dozens of county papers. Searchable full text for obituaries, court notices, and marriage announcements.

Published since 1925, this quarterly journal contains abstracts of NJ records (wills, deeds, church records, cemetery inscriptions, tax lists) and original genealogical research. Essential for NJ-specific genealogical scholarship. Back issues contain thousands of record abstracts not available elsewhere.

military_records.source_name: NJ State Archives — Military Records Collection
military_records.description: NJ military records from the colonial wars through Vietnam. Includes Adjutant General records, militia rolls, Revolutionary War service records and damage claims, War of 1812 records, Civil War regimental records, WWI bonus applications, and WWII records. NJ was a major battleground state in the Revolution (Trenton, Princeton, Monmouth).
Digitized NJ military records including pension files, compiled military service records, and unit records. Revolutionary War pension files are especially important for NJ families.

land_and_property.repository: NJ State Archives / Rutgers Special Collections
land_and_property.description: The Board of General Proprietors of the Eastern Division of New Jersey maintained records of proprietary land grants, surveys, and patents from the 1680s. These records are critical for tracing original land ownership in eastern NJ counties. The proprietors still exist as a legal entity.

land_and_property.repository: West Jersey Proprietors Office, Burlington / NJ State Archives
land_and_property.description: The Council of West Jersey Proprietors maintained proprietary surveys, warrants, and patents for western NJ from the 1670s. Burlington holds original records; NJSA has copies. The West Jersey Proprietors also still exist as a legal entity. Critical for colonial land research in Burlington, Salem, Gloucester, Cumberland, and Cape May Counties.

Each NJ county clerk records deeds, mortgages, and other property instruments. The oldest deed records date to 1681 (Burlington and Salem). Many counties now offer online deed index searches. County clerks are the primary repositories for post-proprietary land records.

vital_records.description: New Jersey began statewide vital registration in 1848 — one of the earliest states. Birth, marriage, and death records at NJSA. Pre-1848 events recorded at township level or in church records only. Access restrictions apply to recent records.

vital_records.source_name: NJ Department of Health — Vital Statistics
vital_records.description: Current custodian of NJ vital records. Certified copies of birth, marriage, and death certificates available by mail or in person. Genealogical access to older records at NJSA.
Indexed NJ vital records including marriage records, death indexes, and birth indexes. Coverage varies by time period. Supplements NJSA holdings with searchable indexes.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Dutch Reformed Church Records of NJ
church_and_cemetery.repository: Gardner Sage Library, New Brunswick / FamilySearch
church_and_cemetery.description: Dutch Reformed church records are among the oldest in NJ (from the 1660s). Baptism, marriage, and membership records — many originally in Dutch — cover colonial families in Bergen, Somerset, Middlesex, Monmouth, and Hunterdon Counties. The Holland Society of New York published transcriptions of many early registers.

Society of Friends meeting records for West Jersey (Burlington, Salem, Gloucester, Cumberland Counties). Include births, marriages, deaths, certificates of removal, disownments, and meeting minutes from the 1670s. Quaker records are among the most complete genealogical sources for colonial NJ.

Online cemetery documentation for NJ. Extensive coverage of NJ cemeteries including colonial-era burial grounds, church cemeteries, and municipal cemeteries. Supplement with published cemetery transcription projects from county historical societies.

research_guides_and_societies.description: The primary genealogical society for NJ. Publishes the 'Genealogical Magazine of New Jersey' (since 1925). Maintains research files, hosts lectures, and provides research guidance. GSNJ publications include thousands of abstracted NJ records.

Volunteer-maintained county-level genealogy websites for all 21 NJ counties. Includes transcribed records, cemetery lists, census indexes, and links to local resources. Quality and completeness vary by county.
Comprehensive online research guide for NJ genealogy. Covers record types, repositories, county guides, and research strategies. Updated regularly by volunteers.

historical_counties_extinct.source_name: East Jersey and West Jersey Provinces (1674-1702)
historical_counties_extinct.description: New Jersey was divided into East Jersey and West Jersey from 1674 to 1702. East Jersey (governed from Perth Amboy) included Bergen, Essex, Middlesex, and Monmouth as original counties. West Jersey (governed from Burlington) included Burlington and Salem as original counties. The provinces were united as a royal colony in 1702, but the East/West distinction persists in proprietary land records to this day. No modern counties have been dissolved or made extinct — all 21 current counties have been created by subdivision from the original six.
historical_counties_extinct.notes: NJ has no extinct counties. All 21 current counties were created by progressive subdivision from the six original counties (Bergen, Burlington, Essex, Middlesex, Monmouth, Salem). The East Jersey/West Jersey proprietary distinction (pre-1702) is important for land records but does not represent extinct counties.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Under the 1804 Gradual Abolition Act, slave owners were required to register births of children born to enslaved mothers. These registrations are filed with county clerks and copies at NJSA. NJ was the LAST Northern state to fully abolish slavery — some persons were held as 'apprentices for life' until the 13th Amendment (1865). Records include slave birth certificates, manumission deeds, and advertisements for runaway enslaved persons.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.repository: NJ State Archives / NJ Historical Society
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Colonial-era records documenting slavery in NJ including wills listing enslaved persons, estate inventories, tax ratables showing slaveholdings, and court records. Bergen, Monmouth, Somerset, and Middlesex Counties had particularly significant enslaved populations in the colonial era. The NJ Archives series (published colonial documents) includes scattered slave-related records.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: African American Heritage in NJ — Digital Resources
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Digital resources documenting NJ's African American communities including the Great Migration settlements in Newark, Camden, and Trenton; the free Black community of Lawnside (incorporated 1926, one of the oldest self-governing Black communities in the North); and the Timbuctoo settlement in Burlington County. Rutgers has digitized some related manuscript collections.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Ellis Island processed over 12 million immigrants from 1892-1954. The island is partially in NJ (Supreme Court ruling, 1998). Many immigrants gave NJ addresses as final destinations — Newark, Jersey City, Paterson, Elizabeth, and other industrial cities. The foundation's database is fully searchable by name, date, ship, and destination.
Pre-Ellis Island immigration processing station. Many NJ-bound immigrants arrived through Castle Garden from 1855-1890. Database is searchable and free.

immigration_and_naturalization.repository: NJ State Archives / NARA New York
immigration_and_naturalization.description: Naturalization records from NJ county courts (Court of Common Pleas, Superior Court). NJSA holds indexes and some original records. Major immigrant groups include Dutch (colonial), English Quakers (colonial), Germans/Palatines (1720s-1850s), Irish (1840s+), Italians (1880s+), Jews (1890s+), Poles (1890s+), and many others. NJ was one of the most ethnically diverse states in America.

burned_counties_summary.notes: New Jersey has no burned counties. Record survival is generally excellent. The most significant loss was the 1873 Monmouth County courthouse fire, which destroyed some court records but spared deed and probate records. The 1902 Paterson fire (Passaic County) destroyed municipal and business records but county courthouse records survived. NJ's proprietary land records (East Jersey and West Jersey) are remarkably well preserved from the 1670s-1680s.
